2: Iesoun Christon en sarki elelythota — Jesus Christ having come in the flesh — the perfect participle elelythota (from erchomai) indicates not merely a past event but a permanent state: having come and continuing to be in the state of having-come-in-flesh. The incarnation is not a temporary episode but a permanent mode of existence (the risen Christ is still the enfleshed Christ). This is the anti-Docetic criterion: spirits denying this permanent incarnate state are not from God.
8: ho theos agape estin — God is love — one of only two direct ontological predications of this type in the NT (the other is "God is spirit," John 4:24). The syntax is not reversible: love is not God, but God is love — love is a property of God's essential being, not God's definition from love's perspective. This grounds the entire argument: love's origin (ek tou theou = from God), love's standard (the Son's self-giving), and love's purpose (mutual abiding) all flow from this ontological claim.
16: ho menon en te agape en to theo menei kai ho theos en auto menei — the one abiding in love abides in God and God abides in him — the mutual-indwelling formula reaches its full expression. Meno (abide, remain, dwell) is the signature Johannine verb: vine-and-branches (John 15), Spirit-dwelling (John 14:17), Father-Son indwelling (John 14:10), and now the community's love-abiding. The grammar is concentric: love → God → mutual indwelling; leaving love = leaving God.
18: teleia agape exo ballei ton phobon — perfect love throws out fear — ekballo is the standard verb for exorcism in the Synoptic Gospels. Perfect love casts out fear the way Jesus cast out demons: decisively, authoritatively, completely. kolasis — punishment, torment (not the general word for punishment but the word for painful suffering) — fear has to do with anticipated kolasis, which produces the tormented life of the not-yet-perfected. ho de phoboumenos ou teteleiōtai en te agape — the one fearing has not been perfected in love — fear is diagnostic of incomplete love formation.
19: hemeis agapomen hoti autos protos egapesen hemas — we love because he first loved us — the hoti is causal: the prevenience of divine love is the sole sufficient cause of human love-capacity. There is no human love for God that did not first receive love from God; the entire ethical project of 1 John rests on the initiative of God's love in the sending of the Son. The comparative protos (first) is temporal and logical: God's love precedes, initiates, and enables ours.
1: μὴ παντὶ πνεύματι πιστεύετε — "do not believe every spirit." Πνεῦμα (G4151, lowercase here — created or supernatural spirits, including those motivating false prophets). The imperative πιστεύετε with μή = "stop trusting" or "do not habitually trust." δοκιμάζετε τὰ πνεύματα — "test the spirits." Δοκιμάζω (G1381) = to test by experience, to examine in order to approve or reject — the verb used for testing metals to determine their genuineness. ὅτι πολλοὶ ψευδοπροφῆται ἐξεληλύθασιν εἰς τὸν κόσμον — perfect: "have gone out into the world" and remain there.
3: πᾶν πνεῦμα ὃ μὴ ὁμολογεῖ τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ἔστιν — "every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God." The object shifts: not "Jesus Christ in flesh" but simply "Jesus" — the particular historical person. Μή + present indicative: any spirit whose ongoing, habitual stance is to not acknowledge Jesus. τοῦτό ἐστιν τὸ τοῦ ἀντιχρίστου — "this is (the spirit) of the antichrist." The antichrist's spirit is recognizable by this specific christological deficiency. ἤδη ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἐστίν — "it is already in the world." The eschatological adversary is not only future but presently active.
4: ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστε — "you are of God." The community's origin (ἐκ) is God, contrasted with the false spirits. νενικήκατε αὐτούς — "you have overcome them" (perfect: completed victory with ongoing standing). ὅτι μείζων ἐστὶν ὁ ἐν ὑμῖν ἢ ὁ ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ — "because greater is the one in you than the one in the world." A comparison between two personal powers: the Spirit of God dwelling in believers (cf. 3:24) and the devil / spirit of antichrist operating in the world. The victory rests not in human resistance but in the superior power of the indwelling Spirit.
5: αὐτοὶ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου εἰσίν — "they are of the world." The false teachers' origin and allegiance (ἐκ) is the world. διὰ τοῦτο ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου λαλοῦσιν καὶ ὁ κόσμος αὐτῶν ἀκούει — "therefore from the world they speak, and the world listens to them." Three consecutive κόσμος uses: origin, content, audience all share the same sphere. The world's receptiveness to the false teachers is itself a diagnostic — if the κόσμος as organized opposition to God welcomes a teaching, that is evidence against its divine origin.
6: ἡμεῖς ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐσμεν — emphatic "we are of God" by pronoun position, contrasted with v.5's "they are of the world." ὁ γινώσκων τὸν θεὸν ἀκούει ἡμῶν — "the one who knows God listens to us." Receptiveness to the apostolic witness correlates with orientation toward God. ἐκ τούτου γινώσκομεν τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας καὶ τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς πλάνης — "by this we know the spirit of truth and the spirit of error." Πλάνη (G4106) — wandering, error. Two spirits, identified by their relationship to apostolic truth and their audience.
7: Ἀγαπητοί, ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους — "Beloved, let us love one another." The hortatory subjunctive ἀγαπῶμεν opens the letter's sustained theological treatment of love. ὅτι ἡ ἀγάπη ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν — "because love is from God." The preposition ἐκ: love originates in God; it is not a human achievement transplanted to God. πᾶς ὁ ἀγαπῶν ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ γεγέννηται καὶ γινώσκει τὸν θεόν — "everyone who loves has been born of God and knows God." Two results: born of God (perfect, settled state) and knows God (present, ongoing relationship).
9: ἐν τούτῳ ἐφανερώθη ἡ ἀγάπη τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν ἡμῖν — "in this the love of God was manifested among us." Φανερόω (aorist) — the same manifestation-verb throughout 1 John (1:2; 3:5,8). God's love became visible in a historical event. ὅτι τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ τὸν μονογενῆ ἀπέσταλκεν ὁ θεὸς εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "because God has sent his only-begotten Son into the world." Ἀπέσταλκεν: perfect of ἀποστέλλω — a past sending with present effect. Μονογενής (G3439) — "only-begotten" or "unique" — carries both uniqueness and the relational intimacy of the only son. The sending into the world (εἰς τὸν κόσμον) is costly in the Johannine frame where κόσμος is hostile territory.
10: ἐν τούτῳ ἐστὶν ἡ ἀγάπη — "in this is love" — love's essence is being defined, not merely illustrated. οὐχ ὅτι ἡμεῖς ἠγαπήσαμεν τὸν θεόν — "not that we loved God." Aorist — the focus is on initiative, not ongoing pattern: who acted first. ἀλλ᾽ ὅτι αὐτὸς ἠγάπησεν ἡμᾶς καὶ ἀπέστειλεν τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ ἱλασμὸν περὶ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ἡμῶν — "but that he loved us and sent his Son as propitiation for our sins." Ἱλασμόν (G2434, second and last occurrence in 1 John; cf. 2:2) — the Son sent to be a hilasmos, a propitiation. Both aorists (ἠγάπησεν, ἀπέστειλεν) frame the prior divine initiative as a single historical complex of love-and-sending.
11: Ἀγαπητοί, εἰ οὕτως ὁ θεὸς ἠγάπησεν ἡμᾶς — "Beloved, if God so loved us." The εἰ + aorist indicative is a first-class condition: the reality is assumed as the basis for inference. καὶ ἡμεῖς ὀφείλομεν ἀλλήλους ἀγαπᾶν — "we also are obligated to love one another." Ὀφείλω (G3784) — moral obligation — same as 2:6 and 3:16. The obligation derives from received love of God; it is not a separate ethical principle but the logical output of being loved. Chain: God → loved us → we → obligated to love one another.
12: θεὸν οὐδεὶς πώποτε τεθέαται — "God no one has ever beheld." Τεθέαται — perfect of θεάομαι (G2300), to behold carefully or gaze upon. The same claim as John 1:18 (θεὸν οὐδεὶς ἑώρακεν πώποτε). Πώποτε — "at any time, ever" — emphatic absolute exclusion. Since God remains unseen by direct vision, the love of believers for one another becomes the visible form of his indwelling. ἐὰν ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους, ὁ θεὸς ἐν ἡμῖν μένει — the conditional is real: mutual love is both the sign and the condition of divine indwelling. ἡ ἀγάπη αὐτοῦ τετελειωμένη ἐν ἡμῖν ἐστίν — "his love has been perfected in us" (perfect passive: a completed state). God's love has reached its goal in the community of mutual love.
13: ἐν τούτῳ γινώσκομεν ὅτι ἐν αὐτῷ μένομεν καὶ αὐτὸς ἐν ἡμῖν — "by this we know that we abide in him and he in us." ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος αὐτοῦ δέδωκεν ἡμῖν — "because of his Spirit he has given us." Ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος — "of/from his Spirit." The construction is partitive: God has given us a portion from his Spirit — not the Spirit divided, but the Spirit given in the mode of gift. Perfect δέδωκεν: the giving is complete and its result stands.
14: ἡμεῖς τεθεάμεθα καὶ μαρτυροῦμεν — "we have seen and we testify." Τεθεάμεθα — perfect of θεάομαι, same verb as v.12 for the unseen God: the apostolic witnesses have seen what ordinary observers cannot. Μαρτυροῦμεν — present: the testimony is ongoing. ὅτι ὁ πατὴρ ἀπέσταλκεν τὸν υἱὸν σωτῆρα τοῦ κόσμου — "that the Father has sent the Son as Savior of the world." Perfect ἀπέσταλκεν as in v.9: the sending stands. Σωτῆρα τοῦ κόσμου — the same κόσμος that is hostile to God is also the object of the Son's saving mission.
15: ὃς ἂν ὁμολογήσῃ ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — "whoever confesses that Jesus is the Son of God." The confession is specific and public: ὁμολογέω requires verbal, articulated acknowledgment. The content is the identity of the particular historical person Jesus as God's Son. ὁ θεὸς ἐν αὐτῷ μένει καὶ αὐτὸς ἐν τῷ θεῷ — "God abides in him and he in God." The mutual indwelling formula attached to the confession criterion: Christological confession and divine indwelling are structurally linked.
17:
ἐν τούτῳ τετελείωται ἡ ἀγάπη μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν — "in this love has been perfected with us." Τετελείωται — perfect passive, as in 2:5 and 4:12. Love reaching its telos (goal, completion) with us — not merely in God. ἵνα παρρησίαν ἔχωμεν ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῆς κρίσεως — "so that we may have boldness in the day of judgment." Παρρησία (G3954) — the purpose of perfected love is eschatological boldness.

ὅτι καθὼς ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν καὶ ἡμεῖς ἐσμεν ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ τούτῳ — "because as that one is, so also we are in this world." Ἐκεῖνος — Christ. The present tense in both clauses: as Christ is now (in his glorified state), so we are now in this world — not will be, but are. Our identity in the world is already determined by our identification with him.
20: ἐάν τις εἴπῃ ὅτι Ἀγαπῶ τὸν θεόν — "if anyone says, 'I love God.'" This is the sixth if anyone says pattern in the letter (cf. 1:6,8,10; 2:4,6,9). καὶ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ μισῇ — "and hates his brother." ψεύστης ἐστίν — "is a liar." The argument from the lesser to the greater: ὁ γὰρ μὴ ἀγαπῶν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ ὃν ἑώρακεν, τὸν θεὸν ὃν οὐχ ἑώρακεν οὐ δύναται ἀγαπᾶν — "the one who does not love his brother whom he has seen cannot love God whom he has not seen." Visibility is the pivot: the seen brother is the available, accessible object of love; if the capacity for love fails there, it cannot exist for the unseen God.
21: καὶ ταύτην τὴν ἐντολὴν ἔχομεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "and this commandment we have from him." Ἔχομεν — present: the commandment is in our possession now. ἵνα ὁ ἀγαπῶν τὸν θεὸν ἀγαπᾷ καὶ τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ — "that the one who loves God must also love his brother." The καί (also) ties the two loves together: they are not independent commandments but one linked command. The love of God that does not issue in love of the visible sibling is not the love of the God who is love.
